title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption: Across the millennium from 1000 to 2018, real gross domestic product per capita for major economies remained largely flat before undergoing a rapid vertical ascent in the modern era. For the first eight centuries, the lines for the United Kingdom, Italy, Japan, China, and India hover consistently near the bottom of the scale, generally staying below the $5,000 mark in 2011 US dollars. The United Kingdom and Italy show minor fluctuations between 1300 and 1800, yet the overall trajectory remains horizontal. A distinct inflection point occurs around 1800 for the United Kingdom, followed by similar upward turns for Italy and Japan in the late 19th and 20th centuries. These three nations climb steeply, with Japan and the United Kingdom reaching the $35,000 to $40,000 range by 2018. China and India maintain low values until the late 20th century, at which point their lines curve sharply upward, with China approaching $15,000 and India rising toward $7,000 by the end of the period. The visual pattern displays a long period of stagnation followed by an exponential increase in income for all plotted countries.
